questions.stem: A 12-YEAR-OLD BOY with a well-defined NON-SCALY, DEPIGMENTED patch on the DORSUM OF HIS RIGHT FOREARM ONLY. No lesions elsewhere on body.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: HYPOPIGMENTED (not fully depigmented), slightly scaly, ill-defined. Common on cheeks in children. NOT complete depigmentation as seen here.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: SEGMENTAL VITILIGO: complete chalk-white depigmentation, well-defined, unilateral, dermatomal. Common in young patients. Stabilises earlier than non-segmental. KEY: well-defined + complete depigmentation + single segment = segmental vitiligo.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Malassezia furfur infection → HYPOPIGMENTED/hyperpigmented SCALY patches. KOH: spaghetti-and-meatball hyphae. Usually on trunk, not forearm.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Single HYPOPIGMENTED patches with SENSORY LOSS and thickened peripheral nerves. Not completely depigmented.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: HYPERPIGMENTED (brown). Face only. Pregnancy/OCP-associated. Cannot be depigmented patch on forearm of a 12yo boy.

questions.stem: Same case. If lesion were WIDESPREAD involving a wide area of the body — best treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: First-line for LOCALISED (<10% BSA). Not appropriate for widespread (systemic absorption risk, atrophy, striae).

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: May halt progression temporarily but does NOT repigment skin. Unacceptable side-effect profile long-term.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Excellent for localised vitiligo (especially face/neck, sensitive areas). Not suitable for widespread disease.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: NARROW-BAND UVB = GOLD STANDARD for widespread vitiligo. Stimulates follicular melanocytes → perifollicular repigmentation. 2-3 sessions/week. Safer than PUVA (no psoralen needed). Most effective on face/trunk. a — College of Medicine |

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Targeted for localised, resistant patches. Not for widespread disease.

questions.stem: What is the SOURCE OF REPIGMENTATION in vitiligo?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: FOLLICULAR MELANOCYTES (hair follicle bulge) = primary source. NBUVB stimulates them → proliferate and migrate outward → perifollicular dots. LEUKOTRICHIA (white hair) = follicular melanocytes destroyed = POOR repigmentation prognosis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Epidermal melanocytes are DESTROYED in vitiligo. Cannot serve as repigmentation source.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Cosmetic camouflage only. Not biological repigmentation.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Surgical treatment option but NOT the spontaneous physiological source.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: PIH = HYPERPIGMENTATION after inflammation. Different condition entirely.

questions.topic: Bipolar Disorder — Manic Episode with Psychosis
questions.stem: 16-YEAR-OLD MALE: Caught by police before jumping from bridge. Believes he CAN FLY. Assessment: DELUSIONS + HALLUCINATIONS + ELATED MOOD + 2 SUICIDAL ATTEMPTS. Q1 — Diagnosis

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Schizophrenia = flat/inappropriate mood, NOT elated. Mood elevation is not a feature. ≥6 months required.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Lifelong pervasive patterns. NOT episodic psychosis with elated mood and grandiose delusions.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: BIPOLAR I = ≥1 manic episode. This patient: elated mood + grandiose delusion (can fly) + hallucinations + impulsive suicidal attempts = MANIC EPISODE WITH PSYCHOTIC FEATURES. Mood-congruent psychosis = bipolar.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: MDD = LOW/sad mood. This patient has ELEVATED mood.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Anxiety disorders Q2 — Treatment of Choice
choices.explanation: No psychosis, no elated mood in anxiety disorders.

questions.stem: Mother had GDM (diet-controlled) in previous pregnancy. How to screen for GDM in FUTURE pregnancies?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: GDM is often ASYMPTOMATIC. Prior GDM = 50-70% recurrence. Universal early screening required.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: HIGH-RISK women (prior GDM) need screening EARLIER — at first visit, not only at 24-28 weeks.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: ACOG/NICE: prior GDM → OGTT at FIRST prenatal visit (early screening) + if normal → rescreen 24-28 weeks. 50-70% recurrence justifies early diagnosis and intervention.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: HbA1C detects PRE-EXISTING T2DM, not GDM. Affected by physiological anaemia of pregnancy.

choices.label: E
choices.text: No screening unless risk factors develop
choices.explanation: Previous GDM IS the major risk factor. Waiting = missed diagnosis.

questions.stem: 25-YEAR-OLD UNMARRIED MALE: 3 weeks of GENERALISED PAPULOSQUAMOUS RASH. NON-PRURITIC. Involves PALMS AND SOLES. GENERALISED LYMPHADENOPATHY.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cutaneous T-cell lymphoma. PRURITIC. Chronic progressive patches. Does not characteristically involve palms/soles.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Silvery scaly plaques on extensor surfaces. Usually pruritic. No generalised lymphadenopathy. Palmoplantar psoriasis exists but no lymphadenopathy.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: CLASSIC: NON-PRURITIC papulosquamous rash on PALMS AND SOLES (pathognomonic) + GENERALISED RUBBERY LYMPHADENOPATHY. 6-12 weeks after primary chancre. Treponema pallidum. Sexually active unmarried male = STI context.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Herald patch → Christmas tree trunk rash. Mildly pruritic. DOES NOT involve palms/soles. No lymphadenopathy. Self-limiting. College of Medicine |

questions.topic: Cancer Patient on Chemo + Sudden Dyspnoea
questions.stem: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma, 4th cycle chemo: Sudden SHORTNESS OF BREATH. O2 saturation dropped. What is the next step?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: CXR cannot diagnose PE (often normal in PE). CTPA required. fa — College of Medicine |

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ANCER + CHEMO + SUDDEN DYSPNOEA = PE FIRST. Pancreatic Ca = very HIGH TE risk (Trousseau's syndrome = migratory thrombophlebitis). CTPA = gold standard. f PE confirmed → LMWH (preferred over warfarin in cancer — CLOT trial).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: epsis less likely with abrupt sudden onset dyspnoea. PE far more likely in cancer ontext.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: EVER continue chemo with acute dyspnoea and desaturation. Medical emergency equiring investigation.

questions.stem: 15-YEAR-OLD GIRL: 6 months cyclic lower abdominal pain (CRYPTOMENORRHOEA). No menarche (PRIMARY AMENORRHOEA). Examination: BLUISH BULGING MEMBRANE at VAGINAL INTROITUS.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: pain and NO bluish membrane. HAEMATOCOLPOS: menstrual blood retained behind imperforate hymen → BLUISH

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: BULGING MEMBRANE (distended with blood). Cyclic pain = cyclical menstruation with NO outlet. Primary amenorrhoea despite normal hormonal function. Treatment: CRUCIATE INCISION → drain haematocolpos. No uterus → no cyclic pain, no bulge. Similar to MRKH.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Sinovaginal bulbs (endodermal outgrowths from urogenital sinus) form lower vagina and

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: hymen. Failure of central perforation → imperforate hymen. Müllerian duct fusion failure → uterine anomalies (not hymen).

flag.note: Answer not cleanly captured from the source table — verify the key.

questions.stem: 25-YEAR-OLD FEMALE: Painful blisters over body. Multiple FLACCID BULLAE involving body and ORAL CAVITY. Yellow crusted eroded lesions.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: TENSE bullae + NO mucosal involvement + elderly. Anti-BP180/230. KEY DIFFERENTIATOR: TENSE + NO MUCOSA = Pemphigoid.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: FLACCID bullae + ORAL MUCOSA (50-70% first site) + Nikolsky sign positive + young-middle age. Anti-Dsg3 (mucosa) + Anti-Dsg1 (skin). Suprabasal intraepidermal split.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Superficial variant. SKIN ONLY — no mucosal involvement. Anti-Dsg1 only (Dsg3 = mucosal protection; Dsg1 = skin).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Genetic, from birth. Not an acquired autoimmune disease. Loss of keratinocyte cohesion (anti-desmoglein destroys desmosomes). TOMBSTONE APPEARANCE of basal cells. DIF: IgG intercellular "CHICKEN WIRE" pattern.

questions.topic: Page 15 — Dermatology Case 1: Contact Dermatitis (Investigation + Treatment)
questions.stem: DERMATOLOGY CASE 1: [File page 15 — App-based. Vignette not fully visible in source; case is labelled "E. Contact dermatitis" as the established diagnosis.] Q: Best Investigation? A. KOH | B. WOOD's lamp | C. TPHA | D. VDLR | E. ?? (incomplete in source) Q: Treatment? A. Benzathine pencillin | B. Systemic steroid | C. Topical steroid | D. Systemic antifungal | E. ?? (incomplete in source) ■■ FILE ANSWER vs STANDARD TEACHING OPTION COMPLETENESS: Options E for both Investigation and Treatment are INCOMPLETE in the source file (marked "??"). Best investigation for contact dermatitis = PATCH TEST, which would logically be Option E (not visible). Treatment E also not visible — likely a specific allergen avoidance option.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: KOH (potassium hydroxide) = used to detect FUNGAL infections (tinea, candida). Dissolves keratin, leaving fungal hyphae visible. NOT for contact dermatitis. Would be correct for: tinea corporis, tinea pedis, candidiasis.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Wood's lamp (UV light) = screens for: fungal scalp infections (green fluorescence), vitiligo (bright white), pityriasis versicolor (yellow-gold), erythrasma (coral-red). NOT the investigation for contact dermatitis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: TPHA = confirmatory TREPONEMAL test for SYPHILIS. Completely unrelated to contact dermatitis. Would be correct if diagnosis were secondary syphilis (palms/soles non-pruritic rash).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: VDRL = non-treponemal screening test for SYPHILIS. Used for syphilis screening and monitoring treatment response. Completely unrelated to contact dermatitis.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Patch test (incomplete in file) Treatment — Choice Analysis: Option
choices.explanation: PATCH TEST = GOLD STANDARD for ALLERGIC CONTACT DERMATITIS. Suspected allergen panels applied to back under occlusive tape for 48h → read at 48h and 96h. Identifies specific allergen causing type IV hypersensitivity reaction. Standard panel = TRUE test (nickel, fragrance, rubber, etc.). IRRITANT contact dermatitis = does NOT require patch test (clinical diagnosis).

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Benzathine penicillin G = treatment for SYPHILIS (2.4 MU IM single dose for primary/secondary). Completely inappropriate for contact dermatitis. This option is a distractor testing knowledge of STI treatment. fa — College of Medicine |

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Systemic steroids (prednisolone 0.5-1mg/kg) = used for SEVERE or widespread contact dermatitis that cannot be managed topically. Short course (5-7 days). NOT first-line for localised contact dermatitis.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: TOPICAL CORTICOSTEROIDS = first-line treatment for localised allergic/irritant contact dermatitis. Potency depends on site: face/flexures = mild (1% hydrocortisone); body = moderate-potent (betamethasone valerate); palms/soles = potent (clobetasol propionate). Plus: ALLERGEN AVOIDANCE (most important step).

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Antifungals (fluconazole, terbinafine, itraconazole) = for fungal infections (tinea, candida). Not for contact dermatitis.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Option E text not visible in file. Likely relates to allergen avoidance or emollients/barrier creams.

questions.topic: Neurology Case 3 — Spinal Cord Injury + Bartholin Abscess
questions.stem: NEUROLOGY CASE 3: YOUNG MALE — lower limb weakness + SUDDEN URINE RETENTION after MISSILE TRAUMA. Examination: FLACCID (acute spinal shock phase), AREFLEXIA, SENSORY LEVEL AT T10. OB-GYN CASE: PAINFUL, FLUCTUATING VAGINAL SWELLING at 5-7 O'CLOCK POSITION. Question: diagnosis + cause? Spinal Cord Injury — Lesion Location

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Brainstem lesion: cranial nerve palsies + crossed sensory deficits + respiratory compromise. No sensory level at T10.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Pure LMN = peripheral nerve/anterior horn. Focal weakness, no sensory level. The sensory level at T10 indicates CORD, not peripheral nerve.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: SPINAL CORD INJURY AT T10 LEVEL: (1) FLACCID + AREFLEXIA = SPINAL SHOCK (acute phase, first 24-72h) (2) After spinal shock resolves → UMN signs return (spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski) (3) SENSORY LEVEL at T10 = umbilicus level (4) URINE RETENTION = neurogenic bladder (detrusor areflexia in spinal shock) Thoracic cord = paraplegia (legs affected, arms intact).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Peripheral nerve injury: focal, no sensory level, no bladder involvement typically. Multiple nerve territories would need to be affected.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome: below L2, saddle anaesthesia, flaccid bladder/bowel, LMN signs in legs. NO definite sensory level at T10 — that implies cord, not cauda equina (cauda equina = below cord end at L1-L2).

questions.stem: OB-GYN CASE: Painful, FLUCTUATING vaginal swelling at 5-7 O'CLOCK POSITION. File confirms this is a Bartholin gland case. Q1: Diagnosis? Q2: Cause?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bartholin abscess — Bartholin CYST (not abscess) Option
choices.explanation: BARTHOLIN GLANDS: located at 4-8 o'clock positions (bilateral) at vaginal introitus. Provide lubrication during arousal. BARTHOLIN ABSCESS: blocked duct → infection → fluctuant, TENDER swelling. Unilateral, red, warm, extremely tender. Treatment: MARSUPIALIZATION or Word catheter insertion (not simple I&D; = recurs). Bartholin CYST = non-infected, smooth, non-tender swelling from ductal obstruction. Bartholin ABSCESS = infected, fluctuant, painful, red, warm. This patient has PAINFUL FLUCTUATING = ABSCESS.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Ductal obstruction causes CYST (non-infected, not painful, not fluctuant). The abscess develops when the CYST becomes INFECTED. Obstruction is the predisposing factor, not the direct cause of the abscess.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Bartholin abscesses are not congenital. They are acquired.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: BARTHOLIN ABSCESS CAUSE = bacterial infection (POLYMICROBIAL): (1) Skin commensals: E. coli, Staphylococcus, anaerobes (2) STIs: Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia trachomatis (especially in sexually active women) Treatment: (1) MARSUPIALIZATION = gold standard (creates permanent drainage opening) (2) Word catheter balloon = good alternative for acute abscess (3) Antibiotics (cover STIs + anaerobes) ALWAYS exclude malignancy in women >40y (Bartholin gland carcinoma rare but exists).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Viral infections do not cause classic Bartholin abscess. HSV can cause vulvar ulcers/vesicles but NOT a fluctuant Bartholin abscess.

questions.stem: ELDERLY PATIENT: DECREASED NIGHT VISION but paradoxically notices IMPROVED NEAR VISION while reading his favourite book. Questions: (1) Most common cause? (2) Cause of near vision improvement? (3) Which treatment is useful? (4) Investigation required for lens choice? Q1 — Most Common Cause of his Disease

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: SENILE (AGE-RELATED) CATARACT = most common cause of cataract globally (90%+). Caused by: protein aggregation in lens → opacity → yellowing/browning. Subtypes: NUCLEAR (most common in elderly), posterior subcapsular, cortical. Nuclear cataract: hardened nucleus → amber/brown colour → myopic shift.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: In elderly, cataract is age-related (not truly idiopathic). Idiopathic applies when no cause identified in younger patients.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Congenital cataracts: present at birth. Rubella, Toxoplasma, galactosaemia, Down syndrome. Not the cause in an elderly patient.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Secondary cataracts: from DM (snowflake/subcapsular), steroids (PSC), uveitis, trauma. No such history given here.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Metabolic Q2 — Cause of Near Vision Improve
choices.explanation: Metabolic cataracts: DM most common cause. "Snowflake cataract" in DM. Not the most common overall. Shift)

questions.stem: ENT CASE 2: ELDERLY MALE with PERSISTENT COUGH + HOARSENESS + STRIDOR. Q1: Investigation for diagnosis? Q2: If Glottic CA T1N0M0 — best option to preserve voice? Q3: Most recognised risk factor for laryngeal CA? Q1 — Investigation for Diagnosis

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: DIRECT LARYNGOSCOPY (under GA) + BIOPSY = gold standard for laryngeal pathology. Allows: direct visualisation of larynx + representative biopsy for histology. Histology: squamous cell carcinoma (>95% of laryngeal cancers). Also: assess subglottic extension, cord mobility, arytenoid fixation. Supports staging and surgical planning.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CXR may be done to exclude synchronous lung malignancy but is NOT diagnostic for laryngeal cancer. Persistent hoarseness in elderly male → must visualise larynx.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Resolves with rest Q2 — Voice-Preserving Treatment f
choices.explanation: NEVER assume persistent hoarseness (>3 weeks in adult) will self-resolve. REFER URGENTLY for laryngoscopy. Hoarseness >3 weeks in adult = red flag = 2-week wait urgent referral in UK (NICE 2015). N0M0

questions.topic: Page 25 — Haematology MCQ: Low Platelets — Least Likely Diagnosis
questions.stem: Low platelet level — which of the following is the LEAST LIKELY diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura = autoimmune destruction of platelets by anti-platelet antibodies (anti-GPIIb/IIIa). Classic cause of isolated thrombocytopenia.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura = ADAMTS13 deficiency → microthrombi → platelet consumption → thrombocytopenia. Pentad: MAHA + thrombocytopenia + neurological + renal + fever.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Von Willebrand Disease Type 2B = enhanced binding of abnormal vWF to platelets → platelet clearance → THROMBOCYTOPENIA (can occur). Most VWD types: normal platelet count; Type 2B = exception.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: HAEMOPHILIA A = Factor VIII deficiency. PLATELET COUNT = NORMAL in haemophilia. Haemophilia = COAGULATION CASCADE disorder (intrinsic pathway). aPTT prolonged, PT normal, platelets normal. Bleeding: deep haemarthroses, muscle bleeds (not petechiae). KEY: Haemophilia ≠ thrombocytopenia.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: APS = antiphospholipid antibodies → THROMBOSIS + obstetric morbidity + THROMBOCYTOPENIA (antibodies also bind platelet phospholipids → destruction).
